medication_allergies.if_yes_please_list_the_medications_and_d:
Amoxicillin – developed a diffuse, blotchy red rash over the trunk and legs within 24 hours of starting the medication at age 3, along with mild facial swelling and itching. No trouble breathing, but pediatrician advised stopping the medication immediately and documented as a likely allergic reaction. Rash resolved over several days with oral antihistamine (children’s cetirizine) and topical hydrocortisone cream. 

Ibuprofen – at age 4, after two doses for fever, he developed hives on arms and torso and complained that his lips felt “tingly.” No wheezing or difficulty breathing, but we took him to urgent care, where they advised avoiding ibuprofen and other NSAIDs until evaluated by an allergist. He now uses acetaminophen only for pain/fever. 

Both suspected allergies have been noted in his primary care record. No history of anaphylaxis, but we are cautious and avoid these medications entirely. If exposed accidentally, we have been instructed to monitor closely for hives, swelling of lips/face, or breathing changes and seek medical care immediately.

part_c_other_needs_requiring_assistance_.if_you_checked_any_boxes_in_10_above_bri:
My son, Ethan, is 6 years old and currently in first grade. He receives school-based speech therapy for a mild articulation delay, and he has also been evaluated for attention and anxiety concerns. While he is generally very social and friendly, he sometimes has difficulty with transitions, sitting still for longer group activities, and clearly expressing himself when he gets excited or upset. 

Communication: Ethan struggles with certain consonant blends (for example, “sp,” “st,” and “tr”) and can get frustrated when others do not understand him right away, especially in noisy environments. It helps a lot when adults: (1) get down at his eye level, (2) ask him to slow down and “use his strong speech sounds,” and (3) repeat back what they think he said so he can confirm or correct it. Simple cues like “take a breath and try again” work well. If staff can give him a bit of extra time to answer questions during circle time or group activities, that also reduces his frustration. 

Social/Emotional (Anxiety): Ethan tends to be anxious in new situations or with changes in routine. For example, on the first day in a new classroom, he may cling to an adult, become very quiet, or say his stomach hurts. He is especially sensitive to loud, unexpected noises (like fire drills or very boisterous play). He usually calms down if an adult calmly explains what is happening, gives him a heads-up before transitions (e.g., “In five minutes we’re going outside”), and offers a quiet space for a brief break if he seems overwhelmed. 

Learning and Attention (ADHD – combined type, mild): Ethan has recently been diagnosed with ADHD, combined type. He is not currently on medication. He does best when instructions are broken into one or two simple steps at a time, with visual cues when possible (e.g., pointing to the cubby while saying, “First put your backpack away, then sit on the carpet”). He may fidget or get up during longer seated activities; gentle redirection (“Feet on the floor, eyes on the book”) and offering a preferred fidget item (like a small, quiet squishy toy) helps him stay engaged. He responds very well to positive reinforcement—praise like “I like how you are sitting and listening” or small reward systems (stickers, points toward a preferred activity) motivate him. 

Overall, the type of assistance he will need in care includes: 
- Extra time and patience when he is speaking, with gentle reminders to slow down and repeat words. 
- Advance warning for transitions and changes in schedule to reduce anxiety. 
- The option for a brief, supervised quiet break (e.g., looking at books in a calm corner) if he seems overwhelmed, tearful, or starts to complain of a “tummy ache” due to nerves. 
- Simple, step-by-step directions and visual or physical prompts when possible. 
- Calm, consistent behavior expectations with immediate, specific praise when he follows directions or uses coping strategies (like deep breaths). 
- Redirection instead of punishment when he is fidgety or distracted, and seating him closer to the teacher during group activities if possible. 

These supports are similar to what his current teacher and school counselor use at his elementary school, and they have been effective in helping him participate successfully in class and social activities.
part_c_other_needs_requiring_assistance_.briefly_describe_any_other_type_of_assis:
Ethan may need some additional help during pick-up and drop-off, as transitions between home, school, and the program can be stressful for him. It helps if staff can greet him by name, offer a predictable routine (for example, “First hang up your backpack, then choose a quiet activity like drawing or reading”), and reassure him about the day’s schedule (“After snack we will go outside, then we will come back in for art”). 

He occasionally experiences mild sensory sensitivities—he does not like very tight clothing or tags and may react strongly if something feels “scratchy” or wet (like spilled juice on his shirt). If this happens, he may complain repeatedly or try to remove the clothing. If possible, having access to a spare T-shirt or sweatshirt (which I can send to keep in his cubby) would help staff manage these situations quickly so he can return to activities. 

He can be a bit slow with self-help tasks such as tying shoes or fully zipping jackets, especially when rushed. Gentle reminders and a little extra time are usually enough, but he may occasionally need an adult to quickly help with zippers or shoe laces when transitioning outside. 

Other than the supports described above, he does not require specialized medical equipment, mobility assistance, or one-on-one support. None beyond what is listed here.
